steps.title: Identify the key signal
steps.content: The word “Tellingly” flags the crucial clue: every Mixe-Zoquean word borrowed into Mayan languages comes from the Zoque branch specifically — not the Mixe branch. If the Olmec language (from one branch) supplied those words, then the Mixe and Zoque branches must have already split before Olmec dominance began.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices, confirm the right one
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Coatálan Mixe and the other languages of the Mixe branch likely supplanted the languages of the Zoque branch sometime before 1500 BCE.': If Mixe languages had replaced Zoque languages before the Olmecs, there would be no Zoque words to borrow — this contradicts the evidence. ✗
  • Choice B — 'the Mixe-Zoquean family had already diverged into the Mixe and Zoque branches by the time the Olmecs became the prevailing power in the region.': An early split explains why only Zoque words (not Mixe words) were borrowed: the Olmec language descended from the Zoque branch, and the two branches were already distinct. ✓
  • Choice C — 'the language of the Olmec civilization contributed words not only to Mayan languages but also to other languages in the Mixe-Zoquean family.': The passage says nothing about Olmec words spreading within the Mixe-Zoquean family; this goes beyond the evidence. ✗
  • Choice D — 'the language of the Olmec civilization was likely the founding language of the family that includes Mayan languages.': Mayan languages are unrelated to the Mixe-Zoquean family; they only borrowed words from it. The passage explicitly states this. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
